copy-move-rename.label: Copy, Move, and Rename
copy-move-rename.summary: Use cp and mv to duplicate, organize, and rename files safely.

sketch2.title: Remove only the file you mean

quiz1.title: Purpose of cp
quiz1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `cp` command in Linux?

help.concept: The `cp` command is used to copy files and directories.
help.hint_2: The `cp` command duplicates files or directories.

quiz2.title: Using mv Command
quiz2.prompt: Which of the following are valid uses of the `mv` command?
quiz2.hint: Consider both moving and renaming functionalities.

help.concept: The `mv` command is used to move or rename files and directories.
help.hint_1: `mv` can change a file's location or name.

options.b: Moving a file to a different directory

quiz6.title: Safe File Operations
quiz6.prompt: Which commands are safe to use for organizing files without data loss?
quiz6.hint: Consider commands that do not delete data.

help.concept: Safe file operations involve copying and moving without deletion.
help.hint_1: Copying and moving are generally safe.

quiz3.title: Order of Operations
quiz3.prompt: Arrange the steps to copy a file and then move it to a new directory.

help.concept: The sequence involves copying first, then moving.

tokens.t2: Move the file to a new directory

quiz4.title: Command for Renaming
quiz4.prompt: Fill in the blank to rename a file using the `mv` command: `mv oldname.txt [blank1]`
quiz4.hint: The blank should be the new name of the file.

help.concept: The `mv` command can rename files by specifying a new name.
help.hint_1: The second argument is the new name.
help.hint_2: The new name replaces the old one.

quiz5.title: Copying a Directory
quiz5.prompt: Fill in the blank to copy a directory and its contents: `cp -r sourcedir [blank1]`
quiz5.hint: The blank should be the destination directory name.

help.concept: The `-r` option with `cp` copies directories recursively.
help.hint_1: The destination is where the copy will be placed.
help.hint_2: The destination can be a new or existing directory.

cp-copies.title: Copy keeps the original
cp-copies.bodyMarkdown:
`cp` makes a second copy of a file and leaves the original in place.

Worked example:
```bash
cp inbox/report.txt archive/report-copy.txt
ls archive
```

Line by line: the first command copies `report.txt` into the `archive` folder with a new name. The second command lists the archive folder so you can confirm the copy is there.

mv-moves-renames.title: `mv` moves or renames
mv-moves-renames.bodyMarkdown:
`mv` can move a file to a different folder or rename it in the same folder.

Worked example:
```bash
mv inbox/today.txt inbox/priority.txt
ls inbox
```

Step by step: the first command renames `today.txt` to `priority.txt`. The second command lists the folder so you can verify the old name is gone and the new name is present.

rm-safe-habits.title: Remove only the file you mean
rm-safe-habits.bodyMarkdown: `rm` deletes files, so check the exact filename with `ls` before you use it. In this course, removal stays inside the lesson workspace and should target only the file named in the task.
